metadata.title:
ADDITIONAL VENIRE IS


CALLED FOR WEDNESDAY

OF IA Available for Duty Two Are Employees


of Northern Pacific.

pages.raw_text:
Additional timber must be had
before the grand jury that will In
vestigate the Gordon-Root case Is
set in motion. The Ave superior
court judges met after the adjourn
ment of court today noon, heard a
report from Judge Huneke on the
status of the work of empanelling
the grand jury, and ordered that
another grand jury venire of 20 be
summoned to appear in court We'd
nesday morning.


Three attorneys, F. T. Post, R.
w. Nuzum and p. G. Sullivan, the
last named of Seattle, watched the
work of empanelling the grand jury
on behalf of Judge Gordon. When
Prosecuting Attorney Fred c. Pugh
waived the questioning of the pros
pective grand Jurors the attorneys
for Gordon did likewise. One OF
them afterward complained be-
cause of the close examination to
which the grand jury veniremen
were subjected by the court, insist
ing that the attorneys should con.
duct the examination and not the
court.


1t was plainly evident after the
first few minutes of the examina-
ton OF the grand Jurors that Judge
Huneke Is going into the work of
securing a grand jury in absolute
earnestness.


He inspected the list closely and
took counsel with his associate
judges as to the material remaining
for grand jury service. After all
excuses were heard there remained
but IA names, and some Of these
were men that the court might
want to replace. but there was no
material to replace them with.
Among the list of the IL men re-
naming are two Northern Pacific
trainmen, and 1t Ia thought that
they will not be used In an investi-
cation involving the integrity Of
railroad oflicials.


Great interest was manifested
about the courthouse In the work
of empanelling. the grand jury, the
first Of It's kind to be called in three
or four years. Prosecuting Attor-
ney Pugh realizes that he Is enter
ing upon the stiffest fight of his ca
reer but he has accepted the chal-
lenge Haunted to him by the defiant
attitude of the Great Northern rail
road and proposes to tight It out to
finish. He will keep the grand
jury In session for q Syear, II neces.
sary, and the words of instruction
as to grand jury service by Judge
Huneke this morning corroborated
this statement. The court stated
that the grand jury might be in
session Indefinitely but that II
could adjourn from time to time as
II saw At.


1t is plainly evident that the eff
fort to probe the Gordon case has
developed into contest In which
the court machinery of Spokane
county will be combated by the
Great Northern railroad influence,
Notwithstanding the fact that a
shortage of many thousand dollars
in Gordon's accounts as general
counsel is admitted by the com.
pany. BY keeping an open grand
jury session whenever service is
secured on any OF the Hill officials
within the state he is bound to an
